topic: Building Your Own AI Orchestration Engine: A Tinkerer's Guide to the Railroad System. ~3000 words. This article tells the story of building an open-source orchestration engine from scratch — why I built it, how it works, what I learned, and why I'm giving it away. NOT a product pitch. This is a builder's journal. Section budgets: (1) Why Build Your Own (~500w) — the moment I realized working without a framework was chaos, vibe coder vs architect distinction, the junior developer analogy for AI agents. (2) The Railroad System (~600w) — YAML-defined pipelines, the spec→build→review→test pattern, why each phase gets a fresh agent with no memory of the previous work, scenario-based testing with LLM judge graders. (3) What the Orchestrator Actually Does (~500w) — concrete examples from this week: breaking XL issues into sub-issues, parallel execution, output capture bugs found by dogfooding, 2600+ tests. (4) The Landscape (~400w) — other orchestrators exist (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, Dify), not competing, just tinkering. StrongDM's 3-person team as inspiration. The 19% slower stat from classical AI-assisted development. (5) Can Anyone Build Software? (~500w) — not an engineer but understanding data flows and ERDs, the weaver/carpenter analogy for what's changing in software development, the resistance from developers and why they're partially right but missing the bigger picture. (6) What's Next (~400w) — web UI, visual block builder, MIT license, the never-ending loop of improvement. Why free. Why open source.
author_facts: Day-one ChatGPT user (November 2022). Has been building with AI for over three years. Built an orchestration engine with 2600+ tests over one month (started February 5, 2026). Not a developer by training — background in project management and system architecture. Managed billing systems and authorization flows at a large enterprise. Led development teams. Understands ERDs, data flows, algorithms — can't write code but understands the logic needed to run it. First console at age 4, first computer at age 12. Lifelong tinkerer who dismounts and remounts everything. Treats his AI agent (running on Claude Opus 4.6) as a junior developer — confident, capable, but makes mistakes with tremendous confidence. Previously published 'Intent Engineering: The Missing Discipline in AI Agent Development' and 'The Dark Factory Gap' on connylazo.com. Uses OpenClaw as the runtime for the orchestrator. Built three working executors: Anthropic API direct, OpenClaw sub-agents, and Gemini via local proxy. The orchestrator runs on a Raspberry Pi as a design constraint. Has shipped content pipelines, coding pipelines, security audits, UX audits, and research pipelines through the engine. Learned about value hierarchies the hard way when pipeline v3 killed all humor from his articles. The 24-hour cooldown before publishing exists because time reliably changes his mind. Uses scenario-based testing inspired by StrongDM's dark factory approach. Built the whole thing in one month with an AI partner, going from zero to 2600+ tests, parallel execution, retry logic, structured output parsing, and file write actions. Plans to open-source under MIT license with a visual block builder.
voice_style: Mark Twain deadpan humor. Dry wit delivered with a straight face. The humor comes from stating absurd truths matter-of-factly, not from jokes. Examples from the author's actual published voice: 'Most of them pass.' (after saying 2,322 tests), 'That is not what it became, but the distance between those two descriptions is the entire argument of this article.' (about building an elaborate LinkedIn post generator), 'The pipeline worked perfectly. It successfully automated the removal of everything that made me sound like me.' (about v3 killing humor). The author uses self-deprecation that builds trust: admitting mistakes openly, treating his AI like a junior dev who's confidently wrong. Never sycophantic, never corporate. Talks like a builder at a bar explaining what he's working on — passionate but unpretentious. Uses analogies from everyday life (weavers making t-shirts, carpenters making furniture). Contrasts absurd corporate language with plain truth. The humor must survive every pipeline phase — protect it like factual accuracy.
source_material: Author's voice brief (transcribed from voice message): The author has been building an orchestration engine for one month, starting February 5, 2026. He is not a vibe coder — he's an architect/project manager who understands complex systems from working at a large enterprise managing billing systems, authorization flows, and development teams. He sees his AI agent as a junior developer who makes confident mistakes, which is why he built the framework. The framework enforces: specification → research → build → review → QA → scenario testing. Each phase gets a fresh agent with no memory of previous work — like a code reviewer who hasn't written the code. The orchestrator works with OpenClaw in a hybrid mode using YAML-defined pipelines. He references StrongDM (3 employees, massive output via quality gates and scenario-based testing) and the finding that classical AI-assisted development is 19% slower. His vision: web UI with visual blocks, MIT license, free for everyone. He wants to provide 'the railroad system' — the framework for people who enjoy building but aren't satisfied with vibe coding results. He's a lifelong tinkerer (console at 4, computer at 12, dismounts and remounts everything). He acknowledges this will generate opinions like his translator article did, and he welcomes criticism because he uses it to build. Key themes: understanding > coding, framework > freestyle, quality gates > speed, tinkering as identity. Also reference the published articles 'Intent Engineering' and 'The Dark Factory Gap' for continuity and cross-referencing.
